prompt: Our deploy tool keeps a big nested dict of service config (dicts and lists all the way down) and right now every override in the release scripts is hand-rolled chained indexing that blows up with useless tracebacks. I want a small module `jsonpath.py` we can all share, with three functions that take a dotted path string like "services.web[2].port": get(data, path[, default]), put(data, path, value, create=False), delete(data, path, missing_ok=False), plus a PathError exception for lookups that fail on well-formed paths. Path syntax: dot-separated keys, [N] indexes attach to the key in front of them (chaining like grid[1][0] works, negative indexes work, and a path may start with [0] when the root is a list). Some of our config keys literally contain dots and brackets ("cpu.pct", "disk[0]"), so backslash-escaping inside keys is required: \. \[ and \\ — and a malformed path (empty string, empty segment, unclosed or non-integer index, junk after a ], dangling backslash) must be ValueError ALWAYS, even when a default was passed; don't paper over typos. get with a default returns it for any failed lookup instead of raising. put replaces or adds the final key on an existing dict without needing create; create=True additionally builds missing intermediate dicts along the way, and for lists lets an index equal to the current length append — anything further out of range stays PathError, I don't want it silently padding lists. delete on a list shifts like list.pop; missing_ok=True makes delete a no-op instead of raising. Everything mutates in place. The acceptance contract is test_jsonpath.py — `python3 test_jsonpath.py` must pass and you may not edit it.
